23:from src.polaris_graph.clinical_retrieval.evidence_pool import EvidencePool, Source
24:
25:
26:# Source.source_id defaults to uuid.uuid4() (lowercase hex + dashes) but is
27:# typed as `str`, so callers may supply any reasonable identifier (e.g.
28:# 'src-1', 'pubmed_12345'). Token regex accepts the broader alphanumeric
29:# + dash + underscore form. Bounded to 100 chars so a runaway token can't
30:# DoS the parser.
31:PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(
32:    r"\[#ev:([A-Za-z0-9_][A-Za-z0-9_\-]{0,99}):(\d+)-(\d+)\]"
33:)
34:
35:
36:@dataclass(frozen=True)
37:class ProvenanceToken:
38:    """A parsed `[#ev:src:s-e]` token with bounds extracted as ints."""
39:
40:    source_id: str
41:    span_start: int
42:    span_end: int
43:    raw: str
44:
45:    def __str__(self) -> str:
46:        return self.raw
47:
48:
49:def extract_tokens(sentence: str) -> list[ProvenanceToken]:
50:    """Find every well-formed provenance token in `sentence`.
51:
52:    Returns parsed ProvenanceToken instances in left-to-right order. A
53:    sentence with no tokens returns []. Malformed candidates that LOOK
54:    like tokens but don't match the regex (e.g. uppercase hex,
55:    `[#ev:abc:bad-bounds]`) are silently skipped — strict_verify is the
56:    layer that rejects sentences for missing/invalid tokens.
57:    """
58:    out: list[ProvenanceToken] = []
59:    for match in PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.finditer(sentence):
60:        try:
61:            start = int(match.group(2))
62:            end = int(match.group(3))
63:        except ValueError:
64:            continue
65:        out.append(
66:            ProvenanceToken(
67:                source_id=match.group(1),
68:                span_start=start,
69:                span_end=end,
70:                raw=match.group(0),
71:            )
72:        )
73:    return out
74:
75:
76:def strip_tokens(sentence: str) -> str:
77:    """Return `sentence` with every well-formed token removed.
78:
79:    Whitespace around removed tokens is collapsed; leading/trailing
80:    whitespace is stripped. Useful for comparing the human-prose part of
81:    a sentence against the cited span (per CLAUDE.md §9.1 invariant 3-4).
82:    """
83:    stripped = PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.sub("", sentence)
84:    # Collapse internal runs of whitespace introduced by token removal
85:    stripped = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", stripped)
86:    return stripped.strip()
87:

89:def has_any_token(sentence: str) -> bool:
90:    """True iff at least one well-formed provenance token is present."""
91:    return PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.search(sentence) is not None

98:class TokenValidationError(str):
99:    """Reason a token failed validation against the pool. Plain string
100:    enum; used in strict_verify drop_reason mapping."""
101:
102:    UNKNOWN_SOURCE_ID = "invalid_token"
103:    SPAN_OUT_OF_RANGE = "span_out_of_range"

105:
106:def _source_text_length(source: Source) -> int:
107:    """Return the length of the text the token is allowed to reference.
108:
109:    Prefers full_text when available; falls back to snippet length.
110:    """
111:    if source.full_text is not None:
112:        return len(source.full_text)
113:    return len(source.snippet)
114:
115:
116:def validate_token_against_pool(
117:    token: ProvenanceToken,
118:    pool: EvidencePool,
119:) -> str | None:
120:    """Validate a single token against the pool.
121:
122:    Returns:
123:        None — token is valid (CLAUDE.md §9.1 invariants 1+2 met)
124:        TokenValidationError.UNKNOWN_SOURCE_ID — source_id not in pool
125:        TokenValidationError.SPAN_OUT_OF_RANGE — span bounds invalid
126:    """
127:    source = _find_source(pool, token.source_id)
128:    if source is None:
129:        return TokenValidationError.UNKNOWN_SOURCE_ID
130:
131:    if token.span_start < 0:
132:        return TokenValidationError.SPAN_OUT_OF_RANGE
133:    if token.span_start > token.span_end:
134:        return TokenValidationError.SPAN_OUT_OF_RANGE
135:
136:    text_len = _source_text_length(source)
137:    if token.span_end > text_len:
138:        return TokenValidationError.SPAN_OUT_OF_RANGE
139:
140:    return None
141:
142:
143:def _find_source(pool: EvidencePool, source_id: str) -> Source | None:
144:    for source in pool.sources:
145:        if source.source_id == source_id:
146:            return source
147:    return None
148:
149:
150:def get_span_text(token: ProvenanceToken, pool: EvidencePool) -> str | None:
151:    """Return the text of the cited span, or None if token is invalid.
152:
153:    Used by strict_verify to compare sentence content against span content.
154:    Prefers source.full_text; falls back to source.snippet.
155:    """
156:    source = _find_source(pool, token.source_id)
157:    if source is None:
158:        return None
159:    text = source.full_text if source.full_text is not None else source.snippet
160:    if token.span_start < 0 or token.span_end > len(text):
161:        return None
162:    return text[token.span_start : token.span_end]

286:                orig_idx[ne - 1] + 1 if ne - 1 < len(orig_idx)
287:                else len(out)
288:            )
289:            ranges.append((orig_start, orig_end))
290:    if ranges:
291:        # Merge overlapping ranges and apply in reverse (preserves indices)
292:        ranges.sort()
293:        merged: list[tuple[int, int]] = []
294:        for s, e in ranges:
295:            if merged and s <= merged[-1][1]:
296:                merged[-1] = (merged[-1][0], max(merged[-1][1], e))
297:            else:
298:                merged.append((s, e))
299:        for s, e in reversed(merged):
300:            out = out[:s] + _DELIMITER_REDACTION + out[e:]
301:            redactions += 1
302:    return out, redactions

305:def wrap_evidence_for_prompt(
306:    evidence_id: str,
307:    statement: str,
308:    direct_quote: str,
309:    source_url: str = "",

312:    """Wrap evidence in the delimited form the generator expects.
313:

316:    tries to spoof closing tags can't trivially break out.

320:    total_red = r1 + r2

322:        logger.warning(

324:            "from evidence %s",

332:        f"direct_quote: {quote_s}\n"

347:# I-meta-005 Phase 7 (#991): Regime C — COMPUTED numbers. A calc token binds a
348:# rendered number to ONE field of THIS run's quantified model. Grammar:
349:#   [#calc:<model_id>:<spec_hash>:<field>]
350:# placed IMMEDIATELY after the rendered display value (token adjacency). <field>
351:# addresses every computed number: an output (`tco`), a sensitivity point
352:# (`tco@discount=0.06`), or a break-even (`tco.break_even`). spec_hash binds the
353:# token to THIS run's model (a stale/foreign model_id|spec_hash → Regime C FAIL).
354:_CALC_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(
355:    r"\[#calc:(?P<model_id>[A-Za-z0-9_]+):(?P<spec_hash>[A-Za-z0-9]+):"
356:    r"(?P<field>[^\]]+)\]"
357:)
358:
359:# Regime C equality is canonicalize-and-compare (the parsed adjacent number must
360:# re-format to EXACTLY the field's display_value via the shared _canonical_display)
361:# — there is NO numeric tolerance (a tolerance let a magnitude-scaled wrong number
362:# pass, Codex diff-gate iter1 P1-2). Only the modeled-assumption disclosure label
363:# is a named constant here.
364:_MODELED_ASSUMPTION_LABEL = "(modeled assumption)"
365:

 succeeded in 58279ms:
1510:            failure_reasons=failures,
1511:        )
1512:    return SentenceVerification(
1513:        sentence=sentence, tokens=src_tokens, is_verified=True,
1514:        failure_reasons=[], soft_warnings=["regime_c_modeled_verified"],
1515:    )

1517:
1518:def verify_sentence_provenance(
1519:    sentence: str,
1520:    evidence_pool: dict[str, dict[str, Any]],
1521:    *,
1522:    require_number_match: bool = True,
1523:    quantified_models: dict[tuple[str, str], Any] | None = None,
1524:    allow_local_window_fallback: bool = True,
1525:) -> SentenceVerification:
1526:    """Verify every provenance token in a sentence.
1527:
1528:    Checks:
1529:      1. Evidence ID exists in pool.
1530:      2. Span bounds are within evidence direct_quote length.
1531:      3. If require_number_match AND the sentence contains numbers,
1532:         each number must appear in the claimed span text.
1533:
1534:    Phase 7 (#991) — Regime C router: when ``quantified_models`` is supplied
1535:    (run-scoped registry keyed by (model_id, spec_hash)) AND the sentence carries
1536:    a ``[#calc:...]`` token, the sentence is FORCE-ROUTED to
1537:    ``verify_modeled_atom`` BEFORE the Regime-A machinery below and that result is
1538:    returned. ``quantified_models=None`` (default) skips the router entirely →
1539:    Regime A is byte-identical.
1540:
1541:    I-complete-003 iter-2 (#1189) — ``allow_local_window_fallback`` (default
1542:    True, byte-identical to pre-iter-2). When FALSE, the two gap-#18 LOCAL-WINDOW
1543:    rescue sites are DISABLED so the sentence passes ONLY if its OWN bound span
1544:    directly clears the content/numeric floor AND directly entails — no different
1545:    in-row window may rescue it. This is set FALSE from ``_try_reanchor`` so the
1546:    re-anchored token's FINAL BOUND SPAN ITSELF DIRECTLY SUPPORTS the claim; the
1547:    Codex iter-1 P1 leak (a candidate span kept on the back of a DIFFERENT
1548:    entailing window while the token stays bound to the non-entailing span) is
1549:    structurally closed. Production strict_verify keeps the default True, so the
1550:    Phase 0b grounded-prose rescue is unchanged outside the re-anchor accept gate.
1551:    """
1552:    if quantified_models is not None:
1553:        _calc_matches = list(_CALC_TOKEN_RE.finditer(sentence))
1554:        if _calc_matches:
1555:            # Fail-closed sentence-shape rules (brief §1.5: AT MOST one calc number
1556:            # per sentence). >1 calc token would leave the 2nd..Nth number
1557:            # unverified after the 1st is checked; a MIXED [#calc:]+[#ev:] sentence
1558:            # would launder an unverified Regime-A numeric claim through the calc
1559:            # path. Both drop the whole sentence.
1560:            if len(_calc_matches) > 1:
1561:                return SentenceVerification(
1562:                    sentence=sentence, tokens=[], is_verified=False,
1563:                    failure_reasons=["calc_multiple_tokens_in_sentence"],
1564:                )
1565:            if _PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.search(sentence):
1566:                return SentenceVerification(
1567:                    sentence=sentence, tokens=[], is_verified=False,
1568:                    failure_reasons=["calc_mixed_with_ev_token"],
1569:                )
1570:            return verify_modeled_atom(
1571:                sentence, _calc_matches[0], quantified_models, evidence_pool,
1572:            )
1573:
1574:    tokens = parse_provenance_tokens(sentence)
1575:    failures: list[str] = []
1576:    # Phase 0b Delta 3 (I-meta-005, gap-#18): tracks whether the entailment
1577:    # judge failed OPEN ((ENTAILED,"judge_error: ...")). Set in either judge
1578:    # call below; consumed by the ON-mode fail-closed gate near the return.
1579:    # HOISTED to function-body top (build agent EDIT-8 scope check): the
1580:    # entailment block is nested inside `if require_number_match and
1581:    # valid_token_found:`, so an init at the `if not failures:` site would NOT
1582:    # be in scope at the return when valid_token_found is False (e.g. all
1583:    # tokens fail evidence_not_in_pool) -> NameError. Init here so it is always
1584:    # defined at the return.
1585:    judge_error_flag = False
1586:
1587:    # I-perm-004 (#1198) slice 3: when the gap-#18 local-window rescue ACCEPTS a sentence whose
1588:    # narrow bound span did not directly entail, the [#ev] token is RE-POINTED to the rescue window
1589:    # (the genuinely-entailing span) instead of shipping the original mis-pointed span. Captured here
1590:    # at function scope as (evidence_id, start, end); applied at the return. None => no re-point.
1591:    # Gated by PG_SPAN_RESOLVER (default OFF -> byte-identical) and SINGLE-token scope only (the
1592:    # which-token-to-move combinatorics for multi-token sentences are out of scope, matching
1593:    # _try_reanchor v1).
1594:    reanchor_local_to: Optional[tuple[str, int, int]] = None
1595:
1596:    # I-gen-005 Step 3b commit 1: verifier_text strips ALL citation
1597:    # artifacts (provenance tokens + atom_NNN + bare [ev_XXX]) for ALL
1598:    # internal verifier checks. The original sentence is preserved
1599:    # on the returned SentenceVerification.sentence so downstream
1600:    # (atom_refusal_validator, citation resolver) see atom_NNN tokens.
1601:    # Per Codex Step 3b iter-2 P1: leaving atom_NNN in numeric or
1602:    # content-overlap or entailment inputs would falsely drop valid
1603:    # atom-cited sentences.
1604:    sentence_for_numbers = _verifier_cleaned_text(sentence)
1605:
1606:    if not tokens:
1607:        # Sentences without provenance are rejected outright.
1608:        return SentenceVerification(
1609:            sentence=sentence,
1610:            tokens=[],
1611:            is_verified=False,
1612:            failure_reasons=["no_provenance_token"],
1613:        )
1614:
1615:    # Fix-3b: When a sentence has multiple citations (e.g.,
1616:    # [#ev:a][#ev:b][#ev:c]), the decimals in the sentence may come
1617:    # from DIFFERENT cited spans — the ICER trial's 86.6% treatment
1618:    # arm AND the placebo-comparator 47.6% from a different span. The
1619:    # verifier should require that each decimal in the sentence is
1620:    # found in SOME cited span (union), not in every span.
1621:    #
1622:    # Also: placebo-arm numbers (e.g., "vs 2.4% with placebo") and
1623:    # achievement thresholds ("≥5%") are filtered from the set of
1624:    # decimals we require to verify — they're comparator/structural,
1625:    # not the claim itself.

444:def parse_provenance_tokens(sentence: str) -> list[ProvenanceToken]:
445:    """Extract all [#ev:id:start-end] tokens from a sentence."""
446:    return [
447:        ProvenanceToken(
448:            evidence_id=m.group("ev_id"),
449:            start=int(m.group("start")),
450:            end=int(m.group("end")),
451:            raw=m.group(0),
452:        )
453:        for m in _PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.finditer(sentence)
454:    ]

458:# Verification

460:
461:_NUMBER_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")
462:
463:# Decimal-only pattern (e.g., 14.9, 3.4, 44.2). Integers like "STEP 1",
464:# "week 68", "104 weeks" are study/duration markers, not the claim
465:# itself — so we don't require them to appear in the provenance span.
466:# This prevents over-strict verification failures on sentences like
467:# "In STEP 1, semaglutide achieved 14.9%" where only 14.9 is the
468:# evidence-backed claim.
469:_DECIMAL_NUMBER_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+\.\d+")
470:
471:# I-ready-018 FIX-A3 (#1143): a standalone integer expressed as a PERCENTAGE ("50%", "19%",
472:# "50 percent") IS a claimed value, unlike the study/duration markers above ("STEP 1", "week 68",
473:# "104 weeks"). Captures the full number (incl. any decimal part) immediately before %/percent;
474:# the caller subtracts the decimal set so only standalone integers remain. This lets the
475:# decimal-bearing branch ALSO catch a %-claimed integer (the drb_72 03-004 "50% versus 19%" leak)
476:# WITHOUT requiring structural integers beside a decimal to appear in the cited span.
477:_INTEGER_PERCENT_RE = re.compile(r"(-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*(?:%|percent\b)", re.IGNORECASE)
478:
479:# Dose-pattern (e.g., "2.4 mg", "1.0 mg", "0.5 µg") — these are drug
480:# identifiers / dosing descriptors, not the empirical claim. We strip
481:# them from the sentence before extracting claim-decimals so sentences
482:# that repeat the dose alongside a result (very common) verify cleanly.
483:_DOSE_PATTERN_RE = re.compile(
484:    r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*(?:mg|µg|ug|mcg|kg|g|ml|mL|L)\b",
485:    re.IGNORECASE,
486:)
487:
488:# Placebo-comparator phrase. Numbers inside these phrases describe the
489:# comparator arm; they are structural to the sentence, not the claim
490:# being verified. Strip them so the verifier doesn't require them to
491:# appear in the treatment-arm evidence's direct_quote.
492:_PLACEBO_COMPARATOR_RE = re.compile(
493:    r"(?:vs\.?|versus|v\.|compared\s+to|compared\s+with)\s+"
494:    r"(?:approximately\s+)?-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*%?\s*(?:with\s+)?"
495:    r"placebo|"
496:    r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*%?\s+(?:with\s+)?placebo|"
497:    r"placebo\s+(?:group|arm|recipients)\s*(?:achieving|had|showed|with)?\s*"
498:    r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*%?",
499:    re.IGNORECASE,
500:)
501:
502:# Achievement threshold phrase. Numbers in "≥5%", "at least 5%",
503:# "5% threshold" are thresholds, not measurements.
504:_THRESHOLD_RE = re.compile(
505:    r"(?:≥|>=|>|at\s+least|achiev\w+\s+at\s+least)\s*-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?\s*%?",
506:    re.IGNORECASE,
507:)
508:
509:# Gap-2 hedging detector. Superlative / comparative claims that appear
510:# WITHOUT source-anchoring language are flagged as unhedged. Both lists
511:# below are deliberately conservative — false positives waste a warning
512:# but don't break a run (soft check, no drop).
513:_SUPERLATIVE_RE = re.compile(
514:    r"\b(?:largest|highest|greatest|best|most\s+effective|most\s+potent|"
515:    r"leading|superior|top|unparalleled|unmatched|unprecedented|"
516:    r"better\s+than|worse\s+than|safer\s+than|more\s+effective\s+than)\b",
517:    re.IGNORECASE,
518:)
519:_HEDGE_RE = re.compile(
520:    r"\b(?:reported|described|characteriz\w+|noted|found|suggest\w+|"
521:    r"indicat\w+|one\s+(?:review|trial|analysis|source|meta-?analysis|study)|"
522:    r"according\s+to|a\s+(?:meta-?analysis|review|trial|study|analysis)|"
523:    r"observed|show\w+\s+to\s+be|appears?\s+to\s+be|estimated\s+to\s+be)\b",
524:    re.IGNORECASE,
525:)
526:
527:
528:def _detect_unhedged_superlative(sentence: str) -> Optional[str]:
529:    """Return the matched superlative phrase if the sentence is an unhedged
530:    comparative claim, or None.
531:
532:    Heuristic: a sentence is unhedged if it contains a superlative phrase
533:    AND does not contain any of the source-anchoring hedge words.
534:    """
535:    if not sentence:
536:        return None
537:    # Strip provenance tokens so they don't consume characters the
538:    # superlative regex might otherwise miss.
539:    clean = _PROVENANCE_TOKEN_RE.sub(" ", sentence)
540:    m = _SUPERLATIVE_RE.search(clean)
541:    if not m:
542:        return None
543:    # Look for a hedge anywhere in the same sentence.
544:    if _HEDGE_RE.search(clean):
545:        return None
546:    return m.group(0)
547:
548:
549:def _strip_dose_patterns(text: str) -> str:
550:    return _DOSE_PATTERN_RE.sub(" ", text or "")
551:
552:
553:# I-gen-005 Step 1 iter 2 (Codex P1 #2 fix): range-dash safety.
554:# Only U+2212 (MINUS SIGN) is unambiguously a minus. The en/em/figure
555:# dashes (U+2013/2014/2012) are USED as range separators in clinical
556:# tables ("8.12–8.21", "−7.5 kg to −12.9 kg") — converting them to
557:# ASCII `-` makes `_DECIMAL_NUMBER_RE` extract a fake negative
558:# `8.12-8.21` → `{8.12, -8.21}`. Codex iter 1: this is "not
559:# defensible as Step 4 deferral because Step 1 changes numeric
560:# extraction now."

1744:        # numeric-mismatch branches ran. Now we ALSO require at least
1745:        # MIN_CONTENT_WORD_OVERLAP content words (non-stopword, >=3 chars)
1746:        # to appear in the aggregated cited-span text. Zero overlap =
1747:        # unsupported claim, sentence dropped.
1748:        sentence_content = _content_words(sentence_stripped)
1749:        span_content = _content_words(" ".join(aggregated_span_text))
1750:        if sentence_content:
1751:            overlap = sentence_content & span_content
1752:            if len(overlap) < MIN_CONTENT_WORD_OVERLAP:
1753:                ev_ids = ",".join(sorted({t.evidence_id for t in tokens}))
1754:                # Phase 0b Delta 1 (I-meta-005, gap-#18): the narrow cited
1755:                # byte-range may miss content words the FULL cited row supports
1756:                # (the gap-#18 grounded-prose drop). Per brief §3.3 + R1: the
1757:                # floor-clear only PROPOSES a candidate (a bounded <=400-char
1758:                # window in a cited row holding >=MIN content words); the
1759:                # entailment BIND happens DOWNSTREAM — the entailment block
1760:                # below judges the narrow span, and on NEUTRAL the Delta-2
1761:                # bounded-window re-judge accepts iff ENTAILED (else fail-closed).
1762:                #
1763:                # HARD GATE (Codex diff-gate P1 + architect P1, brief HARD
1764:                # CONSTRAINT #5): the floor-clear is gated on the entailment
1765:                # judge being in ENFORCE mode — the ONLY mode where the
1766:                # downstream NEUTRAL/CONTRADICTED bind actually DROPS. Under
1767:                # PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=off the judge never runs, and under
1768:                # =warn the judge runs but NEVER drops (log-only). In both, a
1769:                # content-words-only floor-clear would be the SOLE gate —
1770:                # laundering a drop into a pass with no enforced bind. So Delta 1
1771:                # proposes ONLY when entailment is enforce; otherwise the pre-0b
1772:                # content-floor drop stays (fail-closed). off = no rescue
1773:                # (byte-identical). shadow = log would-propose, still fail
1774:                # (output + spend neutral, NO judge call). enforce-verification +
1775:                # enforce-entailment + window = clear, deferring to the bind.
1776:                _vmode_c = _verification_mode()

1778:                # I-complete-003 iter-2 (#1189) P1: when allow_local_window_fallback
1779:                # is False (the re-anchor accept gate), this full-row content-floor
1780:                # rescue is DISABLED — the bound span's OWN narrow content overlap
1781:                # must clear the floor, so a different in-row window can never
1782:                # rescue a non-supporting candidate span. Default True preserves
1783:                # the Phase 0b grounded-prose rescue byte-for-byte.
1784:                if allow_local_window_fallback and _vmode_c in ("shadow", "enforce"):

1786:                        _entailment_mode as _emode_c,
1787:                    )

codex
There are actually two strict-verifier implementations in play. `multi_section_generator` imports `strict_verify` from `generator.provenance_generator`, and that one validates spans against `ev.get("direct_quote") or ev.get("statement")`, not the clinical `Source.full_text` path. I’m following that implementation because it is the one used by `_run_section`.
codex
A line-level mismatch is emerging: the distiller validates findings using tokens built from `span_start/span_end`, then stores those exact offsets in the ledger; the reduce output survives `_rewrite_draft_with_spans` unchanged because that function only rewrites bare `[ev_XXX]`. I’m checking whether the reducer is forced to copy the MAP claim or allowed to paraphrase it, because that decides whether this is a token-form bug or a span-size/entailment bug.

2057:        elif _emode_j == "warn":
2058:            logger.warning(
2059:                "[provenance] WARN would_fail_closed_on_judge_error "
2060:                "(enforce-mode would drop this sentence)",
2061:            )
2062:
2063:    is_verified = len(failures) == 0
2064:
2065:    # I-perm-004 (#1198) slice 3: apply the gap-#18 RE-POINT. Only when the sentence is actually
2066:    # KEPT (is_verified) and SINGLE-token — rewrite the token to the rescue window so the report
2067:    # cites the span that genuinely entails (not the original mis-pointed narrow span). The window
2068:    # was numeric/content-matched AND judged ENTAILED, so the re-pointed token is faithful; the
2069:    # accept verdict is unchanged (relabel only, never a new pass). Flag-gated at capture time.
2070:    final_sentence = sentence
2071:    final_tokens = tokens
2072:    if reanchor_local_to is not None and is_verified and len(tokens) == 1:
2073:        _rev_id, _rev_start, _rev_end = reanchor_local_to
2074:        final_sentence = _rebind_single_token(sentence, _rev_id, _rev_start, _rev_end)
2075:        final_tokens = parse_provenance_tokens(final_sentence)
2076:        soft_warnings = list(soft_warnings) + [
2077:            f"reanchored_local_window:{_rev_id}:{_rev_start}-{_rev_end}",
2078:        ]
2079:
2080:    return SentenceVerification(
2081:        sentence=final_sentence,
2082:        tokens=final_tokens,
2083:        is_verified=is_verified,
2084:        failure_reasons=failures,
2085:        soft_warnings=soft_warnings,
2086:        judge_error=judge_error_flag,
2087:    )
2088:

should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a outputs\audit_2026_05_18\all_issues.json:1:[{"body":"## Problem\n\nClaude repeatedly ends a turn with a prose status/next-step report instead of\nthe next tool call, after finishing a unit of work (a PR merge, a grounded\nscan, \"the next step is X\"). The user has flagged this 5+ times\n(2026-05-07 → 2026-05-18: \"why you pause here? any valid reason? figure out a\nway to resolve this constant pausing behaviour\").\n\nEvery prior fix was a *rule* (CLAUDE.md §8.1 banned STATUS blocks; §8.2 banned\nprose-between-merge-and-branch; §8.3.10 banned named rationalizations) — and\neach failed because the pause is a decision made at stop-time and the agent\nrationalizes a fresh exception. Per `failure_28_commits`: \"promises don't\nwork; structural removal does.\" A rule the agent must remember and apply is\nnot structural. The only structural mechanism is one **external to the agent's\nin-the-moment judgment** — a Stop hook.\n\n## Proposed mechanism\n\nA Claude Code `Stop` hook (`.claude/hooks/`, project-scoped per\n`stop_hook_must_be_project_scoped`) that, when the agent attempts to end a\nturn, decides allow-vs-block:\n\n- **Allow the stop** if any of: the last message is a user turn awaiting a\n  reply; a `state/halt_*` marker exists; the transcript shows a Codex iter-5\n  REQUEST_CHANGES; `state/active_issue.json` shows no in-progress work.\n- **Block the stop** (return the `block` decision + a reason) if there is\n  in-progress issue-queue work and none of the allow conditions hold — inject\n  \"continue: the next output is a tool call advancing the issue, not prose.\"\n\n## Hard constraints (this area is delicate)\n\n- **Loop-safety is mandatory.** A Stop hook that always blocks creates an\n  infinite loop. It must bound the number of consecutive blocks and/or only\n  block when the transcript shows a *just-completed* sub-step. See\n  `feedback_no_unbounded_polling_loops`.\n- Per #524 (I-pin-001): the prior `stop_hook_v3.py` canonical-pin check was\n  **deliberately neutralized** for false-firing on Windows CRLF. A\ndetached fetch-backend task whose Playwright cleanup (`_try_crawl4ai` →\n`_safe_close_crawler` → `__aexit__`) is genuinely wedged would therefore\ndelay — in the theoretical worst case, indefinitely — `asyncio.run`\n**teardown**, *after* all artifacts and the manifest are already written.\n\nIn practice Playwright browser cleanup is slow-worst-case, not\ninfinite-worst-case (CDP has internal timeouts; the browser subprocess is\nSIGTERM'd by Playwright's own process management), so the detached task is\nexpected to die within a bounded time. Observed in the I-gen-003 V4 Pro smoke: a single section-outline call produced `content=1791 bytes` + **`reasoning=17521 bytes`** (4790 reasoning tokens vs 5388 output tokens). The reasoning trace is roughly 3× the size of the final content.\n\n**Right now POLARIS discards the reasoning trace entirely.** `multi_section_generator._call_section` (and the outline/repair call sites) return `(response.content, input_tokens, output_tokens)` — `response.reasoning` is dropped on the floor. `LLMResponse` carries a `.reasoning` field but nothing in the generator or audit_ir persists it. A reviewer auditing a POLARIS report cannot see how the model reasoned its way to the cited prose.\n\nFor an audit-grade clinical tool this is a transparency hole: the reasoning IS evidence of process. It must be captured, stored SEPARATELY from the output content (never mixed into report.md / verified_text), and surfaced in the audit bundle.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n1. **Capture** — `_call_section`, the outline call, and the I-bug-108 sentence-repair call each return / thread through `response.reasoning` alongside `content`. `SectionResult` (and the outline result) gain a `reasoning_trace` field.\n2. **Store separately** — per run, write a `reasoning_trace.jsonl` (or per-section files) into the run output dir, ONE record per LLM call: `{section, call_type (outline|section|repair|regen), attempt_n, model, reasoning_text, content_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_tokens, timestamp}`. The reasoning text is NEVER merged into `report.md`, `verified_text`, or the verified-sentence stream — those stay content-only.\n3. **Manifest + bundle** — `manifest.json` references the reasoning trace file(s); the audit bundle (`bundle.tar.gz`) includes them so an external reviewer gets the reasoning alongside the report + provenance.\n4. **Transparency surface** — `/transparency` (or the bundle README) documents that the reasoning trace is captured + where it lives + that it is model-process evidence, not verified claims. (Frontend Inspector rendering of the trace MAY be a follow-up — the core requirement here is capture + separate storage + bundle inclusion.)\n5. **Separation invariant** — a test asserts the reasoning text never appears in `report.md` / verified prose, and that `strict_verify` is never run against reasoning text. Reasoning is process-transparency, NOT a claim source.\n6. **Works for all models** — for non-reasoning-first models `response.reasoning` is usually empty/None; the trace record still gets written (empty reasoning_text) so the artifact schema is uniform. No crash, no-op when reasoning absent.\n7. Codex diff review APPROVE.\n\n## Depends on\n\n- I-gen-003 (GH#495) — V4 Pro must actually run as the generator first. With no upper bound, pip's resolver pulls latest-1.x of each package, hits the langchain 1.x ecosystem's internal version coupling, and backtracks through the entire release history trying to find a compatible set — effectively a hang.\n\nThis did not surface when I-carney-005 shipped Dockerfile.v6 because langchain 1.x had not been released / the resolver still converged then. outputs\audits\I-bug-092\claude_audit.md:1:# Claude Audit — I-bug-092 (entailment judge as 6th strict_verify check)
outputs\audits\I-bug-092\claude_audit.md:12:The 2026-05-09 line-by-line content audit (`outputs/audits/v32_baseline_content_audit/CROSS_REVIEW.md`) found that the V3.2-Exp + Gemma 4 31B baseline tirzepatide report has **1 fabricated mechanistic claim (M2)**, **1 specificity inflation (C2)**, and **1 unverifiable specificity claim (C1)** — all of which passed the existing 5 mechanical strict_verify checks (token presence, token validity, span bounds, decimal subset, content-word overlap).
outputs\audits\I-bug-092\claude_audit.md:14:The architectural diagnosis: strict_verify enforces **provenance presence** (token format + lexical overlap), not **provenance correctness** (semantic entailment between sentence and span). The current design assumes lexical overlap implies content fidelity. Empirically with capable generators, this assumption breaks — they generate plausible-sounding extensions of the source's topic that share words but introduce unsourced facts.
outputs\audits\I-bug-092\claude_audit.md:18:Adds check 6 to `strict_verify.verify_sentence()`: an LLM-as-judge entailment call that asks whether the cited span semantically ENTAILS the sentence's specific claims. Gated by `PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT={off,warn,enforce}` defaulting to `off` so production behavior is unchanged until an operator opts in. Default judge model is `google/gemma-4-31b-it` (the existing two-family evaluator); custom model via `PG_ENTAILMENT_MODEL`.
outputs\audits\I-bug-092\claude_audit.md:26:| §9.1.3 strict verify | This PR EXTENDS strict_verify with a 6th check; mechanical checks (a)-(e) unchanged and run first. | ✓ extended, not replaced |

outputs\audits\beatboth5\FULL_BUG_LIST.md:4:questions (drb_72 workforce, drb_75 metal-ions/CVD, drb_76 gut-microbiota/CRC,
outputs\audits\beatboth5\FULL_BUG_LIST.md:14:The run has **one confirmed P0 faithfulness leak** and **one dominant P0 completeness collapse**, and they are independent. The faithfulness leak: 26 of 39 claims that the binding 4-role (D8) verifier marked **UNSUPPORTED** ship as asserted, cited prose — verified in rendered output, not inferred — because the redactor only redacts "material" (S0/S1/S2) claims and every claim that covers no pre-registered contract entity silently defaults to S3 ("observe-only, never redacted"). In drb_76 this puts UNSUPPORTED clinical-safety guidance ("current evidence advises against routine probiotic use in immunocompromised patients") into the Safety section with zero redaction tombstones. This is lethal-class per §-1.1 and is the highest-impact fix regardless of everything else. The dominant completeness lever is a single mis-implemented timeout: `parallel_fetch` anchors every one of 740 fetch futures to **one shared submit-time deadline** (`submit_now + per_task_timeout`) with only 8 workers, so 85–92% of candidates are batch-killed as "TIMEOUT" before they ever get a worker slot — they are queue-starvation, not network failures. That single bug collapses the evidence pool from ~740 candidates to 9–34 rows and starves every downstream gate (coverage 0.167–0.40, must-cover safety entities missing, corroboration dark, frame curator-gaps). Most other "P0/P1" findings in the lanes are **consequences** of that fetch collapse and must be re-measured after it is fixed, not fixed independently. Faithfulness and completeness are kept strictly separate below: a thin-but-honest report is a completeness problem; an unsupported sentence shipping as a fact is a faithfulness problem, and only the latter can hurt a patient directly.
outputs\audits\beatboth5\FULL_BUG_LIST.md:28:- **Grounding (output-level, not code-inference).** Across all 5: 39 UNSUPPORTED final verdicts, 13 redacted, **26 leaked** (`UNSUPPORTED − redacted_claim_ids − already_absent_claim_ids`); `already_absent_claim_ids=[]` on all 5 (redactor never even checked these were absent). drb_76 Safety section (claim prefix `03-` = 4th outline slot "Safety") renders **zero** redaction tombstones yet ships UNSUPPORTED cited safety guidance: "current evidence advises against routine probiotic use in patients with central venous catheters, immunosuppression, or critical illness.[4]" and "The Finnish study indicates that probiotic use should be considered carefully…[4]". outputs\audits\beatboth8\FAILURE_AUDIT.md:104:`src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py`, `_EntailmentJudge.judge` :142-287. No retry loop. `self._client.post(...)` at :206 (RemoteProtocolError here) and `content = data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]` at :266 (IndexError when `choices:[]`), plus a bad/parse verdict, are all swallowed by the broad `except Exception` at :281, returning `("ENTAILED", "judge_error: ...")`. `BudgetExceededError` is correctly re-raised first at :277-280. **Existing mitigation (not a gap):** both consumers detect the `judge_error:` prefix and FAIL CLOSED in enforce mode — `strict_verify.py:295-301` keys on the prefix alone; `provenance_generator.py:1795-1796`+:1899-1900 set `judge_error_flag` on `verdict=="ENTAILED" AND reason.startswith("judge_error:")`, consumed at :1946-1962 to drop. So the residual problem is **OVER-DROP** (a salvageable verdict lost to a single transient → drb_72-class coverage collapse), and the fail-open is latent-lethal only in warn mode / any future verdict-only consumer. The docstring at :146-147 still calls fail-open intentional and must be updated.
outputs\audits\beatboth8\FAILURE_AUDIT.md:121:   - **DIVERGENCE (flag for Codex):** do NOT flip the sentinel to `('NEUTRAL','judge_error:...')` as a standalone change — `provenance_generator.py:1795` requires `verdict=='ENTAILED'` to set `judge_error_flag`; a bare flip silently bypasses detection and drops the `judge_error=True` field the credibility-disclosure layer consumes. If a NEUTRAL flip is wanted as defense-in-depth for future verdict-only consumers, it MUST be paired with coordinated edits at `strict_verify.py:295-301` (already prefix-only, safe) AND `provenance_generator.py:1795`+:1899 (change to `reason.startswith('judge_error:')` alone). Recommend keeping ENTAILED+prefix for this fix.

Population: more than 1.7 million participants.[1] Outcome: colorectal cancer.[1] Dose response: 0.90 (0.86 to 0.94) for each 10 g/day intake.[1] The pooled analysis included more than 1.7 million participants.[1] In the dose-response analysis, each 10 g/day increment in intake was associated with a relative risk of 0.90.[1] The 95% confidence interval for this estimate ranged from 0.86 to 0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. Three high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree sharply on the effect on ttr (relative difference 12275.0%), on ttr at 0.5 mg (25.0% difference), and on weight loss (212.5% difference), indicating substantial inconsistency in key outcomes. outputs\audits\I-redact-001\redaction_fixture.json:658:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]\n- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]\n- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. outputs\audits\I-redact-001\redaction_fixture.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. logs\session_log.md:3374:- ACTION: M-2 content-starvation root cause diagnosed and fixed. Added verification_details.json artifact (re-runs strict_verify on the persisted rewritten_draft to surface per-sentence drop reasons). Categorized the 24 drop-reasons on m2_diag_clinical: 11 no_content_word_overlap, 7 number_not_in_any_cited_span, 3 no_integer_overlap, 3 no_provenance_token. Found the defect in src/polaris_graph/generator/live_deepseek_generator.py::_rewrite_draft_with_spans: no-decimal sentences defaulted to span=(0, 200) (often the abstract title/header) and decimal sentences got ±30-char spans around the first decimal found — both frequently fail the content-word-overlap check that was added LATER. Fixed by introducing _find_best_span_for_sentence: a content-aware sliding-window finder (default 500 chars, stride 100, PG_PROVENANCE_SPAN_WINDOW / PG_PROVENANCE_SPAN_STRIDE env overrides) that hard-requires every sentence-decimal in the window AND maximizes content-word overlap — the exact criteria strict_verify uses.
