See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[2]\n\n### Prebiotic / dietary-fiber intake and colorectal-cancer risk\n\nExposure: dietary fibre and whole grains.[1] Population: more than 1.7 million participants.[1] Outcome: colorectal cancer.[1] Dose response: 0.90 (0.86 to 0.94) for each 10 g/day intake.[1] The pooled analysis included more than 1.7 million participants.[1] In the dose-response analysis, each 10 g/day increment in intake was associated with a relative risk of 0.90.[1] The 95% confidence interval for this estimate ranged from 0.86 to 0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. Three high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree sharply on the effect on ttr (relative difference 12275.0%), on ttr at 0.5 mg (25.0% difference), and on weight loss (212.5% difference), indicating substantial inconsistency in key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, potentially omitting earlier foundational research and limiting the temporal scope of the analysis.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 feb9fd7172651bfc...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 21}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 375 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 365 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. Actual distribution: T1=14%, T2=1%, T3=0%, T4=9%, T6=5%, T7=5%, UNKNOWN=66%.\nCorpus adequacy: decision=proceed, 8/8 thresholds met.\nCompleteness checklist: 6/6 topics covered.\n\n## Capability disclosures\nQuantified trade-off analysis was ENABLED but did not contribute to this report (no verified quantified output); 399 sourced numbers were extracted but not modeled into a verified quantified comparison.\n\n## Contradiction disclosures\nThe contradiction detector flagged 3 numeric disagreements across the evidence pool. Most are extraction artifacts produced by grouping different measured endpoints, units, sub-populations, time windows, or comparators under the same subject/predicate label. The detector does NOT adjudicate by endpoint, population, timepoint, or source tier; raw detector output is available in `contradictions.json`. .\tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:658:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]\n- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]\n- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. tests\fixtures\drb72\report.md:14:- **Background.** Historical analysis of technological change reveals that automation displaces labor by enabling capital to perform tasks previously done by workers, a displacement effect that reduces the labor share in value added.[2]
tests\fixtures\drb72\report.md:44:Historical analysis of technological change reveals that automation displaces labor by enabling capital to perform tasks previously done by workers, a displacement effect that reduces the labor share in value added.[2] However, this displacement is counterbalanced by the creation of new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage, generating a reinstatement effect that raises labor demand and the labor share.[2] The recent wave of generative AI introduces new dynamics; a study of 5,172 customer-support agents found that access to a generative AI-based conversational assistant increased worker productivity by 15% on average, with the largest gains among less experienced and lower-skilled workers.[6] At the firm level, a cross-sectional analysis of 5,851 German firms revealed that approximately 7% (409 firms) had adopted AI by 2018, with an average AI intensity of 12.9% among users.[8] AI-using firms in that sample reported higher sales growth (5.4% versus 3.7% for non-users) and were more likely to engage in continuous R&D (50% versus 19%).[8] Among AI adopters, 60% used AI in products or services, 56% for process automation, 34% for data analysis, and 22% for client interaction.[8] Related research on Industry 4.0 patents found that an additional such patent increased firms' sales by 8.3%, with the effect diminishing by firm size.[8] The slower employment growth observed in recent decades has been attributed to an acceleration of the displacement effect, particularly in manufacturing, a weaker reinstatement effect, and slower growth of product.[2]
tests\fixtures\drb72\report.md:48:Acemoglu and Restrepo's task-based framework posits that automation displaces labor by enabling capital to replace workers in existing tasks, reducing the labor share and potentially labor demand, while the creation of new tasks reinstates labor by shifting the task content of production in favor of workers.[2] Autor similarly emphasizes that automation substitutes for labor but also complements it, raising output and demand for labor, and that commentators often overstate substitution while ignoring complementarities.[1] One study of US commuting zones found that each additional industrial robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%.[4] These negative effects were robust and distinct from other capital and technologies, and areas most exposed to robots after 1990 showed no differential trends beforehand.[4] As noted under Background, a generative AI assistant increased productivity by 15% on average[6] The productivity gains were heterogeneous: less experienced and lower-skilled workers improved both speed and quality, while the most experienced and highest-skilled workers saw only small speed gains and small declines in quality.[6] AI assistance also facilitated worker learning, improved English fluency among international agents, and led to customers being more polite and less likely to request a manager.[6] Autor notes that recent decades have seen labor market polarization, with wage gains concentrated at the top and bottom of the skill distribution, though he argues this polarization is unlikely to persist.[1] The interplay between machine and human comparative advantage allows computers to substitute for routine, codifiable tasks while amplifying human advantages in problem-solving, adaptability, and creativity.[1] Overall, the evidence indicates that while AI and automation displace some jobs, they also create new tasks and complement human labor, with net effects depending on the balance between displacement and reinstatement and on the skill level of workers.[2][1][6] Their empirical decomposition of industry-level data suggests that the slower growth of employment over the last three decades is accounted for by an acceleration in the displacement effect, especially in manufacturing, a weaker reinstatement effect, and slower growth of product.[2] Behrens and Trunschke (2020) found that an additional "4.0 patent" increased sales by 8.3% for German firms, with the effect diminishing by firm size.[8]

Population: more than 1.7 million participants.[1] Outcome: colorectal cancer.[1] Dose response: 0.90 (0.86 to 0.94) for each 10 g/day intake.[1] The pooled analysis included more than 1.7 million participants.[1] In the dose-response analysis, each 10 g/day increment in intake was associated with a relative risk of 0.90.[1] The 95% confidence interval for this estimate ranged from 0.86 to 0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. Three high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree sharply on the effect on ttr (relative difference 12275.0%), on ttr at 0.5 mg (25.0% difference), and on weight loss (212.5% difference), indicating substantial inconsistency in key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, potentially omitting earlier foundational research and limiting the temporal scope of the analysis.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 feb9fd7172651bfc...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 21}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 375 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 365 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:658:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]\n- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]\n- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. tests\fixtures\drb72\evidence_pool.json:7:    "direct_quote": "We present a framework for understanding the effects of automation and other types of technological changes on labor demand, and use it to interpret changes in US employment over the recent past. At the center of our framework is the allocation of tasks to capital and labor\u2014the task content of production. Automation, which enables capital to replace labor in tasks it was previously engaged in, shifts the task content of production against labor because of a displacement effect. As a result, automation always reduces the labor share in value added and may reduce labor demand even as it raises productivity. The effects of automation are counterbalanced by the creation of new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage. The introduction of new tasks changes the task content of production in favor of labor because of a reinstatement effect, and always raises the labor share and labor demand. We show how the role of changes in the task content of production\u2014due to automation and new tasks\u2014can be inferred from industry-level data. Our empirical decomposition suggests that the slower growth of employment over the last three decades is accounted for by an acceleration in the displacement effect, especially in manufacturing, a weaker reinstatement effect, and slower growth of productivity than in previous decades.",
tests\fixtures\drb72\evidence_pool.json:60:    "direct_quote": "We study the effects of industrial robots on US labor markets. We show theoretically that robots may reduce employment and wages and that their local impacts can be estimated using variation in exposure to robots\u2014defined from industry-level advances in robotics and local industry employment. We estimate robust negative effects of robots on employment and wages across commuting zones. We also show that areas most exposed to robots after 1990 do not exhibit any differential trends before then, and robots\u2019 impact is distinct from other capital and technologies. One more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%.",

For more information, please refer to our Policies page or contact lseresearchonline@lse.ac.uk AI and Jobs: Evidence from Online Vacancies\u2217 Daron Acemoglu MIT and NBER David Autor MIT and NBER Jonathon Hazell LSE Pascual Restrepo Boston University and NBER October 2021 Abstract We study the impact of AI on labor markets using establishment-level data on vacancies with detailed occupation and skill information comprising the near-universe of online vacancies in the US from 2010 onwards. We classify establishments as \u201cAI exposed\u201d when their workers engage in tasks that are compatible with current AI capa- bilities. We document rapid growth in AI \n\n[...]\n\n an establishment-level data set of AI activity based on the near-universe of US online job vacancy postings and their detailed skill require- ments from Burning Glass Technologies (Burning Glass or BG, hereafter) for the years 2007 and 2010 through 2018.2 We start with a task-based perspective, linking the adoption of AI and its possible impli- cations to the task structure of an establishment. This perspective emphasizes that current applications of AI are capable of performing speci\ufb01c tasks and pre\n\n[...]\n\nlishment, industry and occu- pation levels. Section 8 concludes. Additional robustness checks and empirical results are presented in the Appendix. 2 Theory In this section, we provide a model that motivates our empirical approach and interpretation. 2.1 Tasks, Algorithms and Production Establishment e\u2019s output, ye, is produced by combining the services, ye(x), of tasks x \u2208Te \u2282 T with unit elasticity (i.e., a Cobb-Douglas aggregator): ln ye = ln Ae + Z Te \u03b1(x) ln ye(x)dx, (1) where T is the set of f\n\n[...]\n\nthe advances in AI take place. This mea- sure represents the share of tasks performed in an establishment that can now be performed by AI-powered algorithms.6 We next explore how advances in AI impact AI activity and the demand for (non-AI) workers. 2.2 Task Structure and AI Adoption To illustrate how the task structure determines AI adoption, we follow Acemoglu and Autor (2011) and Acemoglu and Restrepo (2018, 2019a) and assume that \u03c3 = \u221e, so that algorithms 5This assumption can be relaxed in vari\n\n[...]\n\nhey increase or 7This expression also clari\ufb01es that when the passthrough rate is less than 1/\u03b5e, the establishment\u2019s price increases su\ufb03ciently that output does not expand and thus employment always declines. 8 reduce their hiring of non-AI workers. 2.4 Human-Complementary AI We have so far not considered human-complementary e\ufb00ects of AI. The possibility that AI will complement workers engaged in exposed tasks can be captured by assuming that \u03b1(x) increases for exposed tasks (see eq. 1) or, alterna\n\n[...]\n\nthus suggests that the current generation of AI technologies is predominantly task-replacing and generates only modest productivity gains.8 It remains possible that other AI technologies than the ones we are proxying here could have di\ufb00erent e\ufb00ects. 2.5 Measuring Exposure to AI Propositions 1 and 2 show that we should see the e\ufb00ects of advances in AI in establishments with task structures that make them highly exposed to AI. Di\ufb00erences in exposure are, in turn, driven by the di\ufb00erent task structure\n\n[...]\n\nxt describe the BG data, document that it is broadly representative of employment and hiring trends across occupations and industries, present our AI exposure indices, and document their distribution across occupations and their evolution over time. 3.1 Burning Glass Data Burning Glass (BG) collects data from roughly 40,000 company websites and online job boards, with no more than 5% of vacancies from any one source. BG applies a deduplication algorithm and converts the vacancies into a form amenab\n\n[...]\n\nent weighted average of the occupation AI exposure measure: P o\u2208Oe OExpo\u2113o P o\u2208Oe \u2113o = P o\u2208Oe R x\u2208T o\u2229T A \u2113(x)dx R x\u2208T o \u2113(x)dx \u2113o P o\u2208Oe \u2113o = R x\u2208Te\u2229T A \u2113(x)dx R x\u2208Te \u2113(x)dx = Exposure to AIe. 10 is the downturn in BG postings data between 2015 and 2017.10 Appendix Figure A1 shows that over the 2010-2018 period, the occupational and industry composition in BG is closely aligned with both overall occupation employment shares from the OES and with industry vacancy shares from JOLTS.11 We make use of Bur\n\n[...]\n\nector 54). The former includes various information technology industries, likely to be selling AI products, while the latter contains industries such as management consultancy, likely to be integrating AI into other industries\u2019 production processes. 3.2 AI Indices We study three measures of AI exposure. Each is assigned at the six-digit SOC occupation level, and each is designed to capture occupations concentrating in tasks that are compatible with the current capabilities of AI technologies. The \ufb01\n\n[...]\n\nthe subsequent two panels present results for our other measures of AI exposure. We estimate regression models based on (6), with the left-hand side variable de\ufb01ned as the change in the inverse hyperbolic sine of AI vacancies between 2010-12 and 2016-18.17 We focus on weighted speci\ufb01cations, using baseline establishment vacancies as weights. In the text, we report heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors that allow for arbitrary cross-sectional correlation across the establishments of each \ufb01rm, and c\n\n[...]\n\n. 17The inverse hyperbolic sine transformation is given by: ln \u0010 x + p (x2 + 1) \u0011 . For small values of x, this approximates a proportional change, but is well de\ufb01ned when x = 0, which is a frequent occurrence in our sample of establishments. 14 \u03b2 = 15.96 is precisely estimated (SE = 1.73) and shows a sizable association between AI exposure and AI vacancies. This estimate implies that a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure\u2014which corresponds to the di\ufb00erence between \ufb01nance and mining and all extraction\u2014is associated with approximately a 16% increase in AI vacancies. The remaining columns explore the robustness of this relationship. Column 2 controls for \ufb01rm size decile and commuting zone \ufb01xed e\ufb00ects. The coe\ufb03cient estimate of AI exposure declines slightly to 13.82, but is now more precisely estimated. Column 3 additionally adds three-digit (NAICS) industry \ufb01xed e\ufb00ects. Re\ufb02ecting the sizable variation in AI exposure across industries shown in Figure 3 above, these controls are more important for our regres- sions, and they reduce the magnitude of our estimate by about a third, to 9.19, but the standard error of the estimate also declines (to 1.21). Column 4 goes one step further and includes a full set of \ufb01rm \ufb01xed e\ufb00ects, so that now the comparison is among establishments of the same \ufb01rm that di\ufb00er in their AI exposure. The estimate of \u03b2 is similar to the bivariate relationship reported in column 1, 16.53, albeit slightly less precise, since all of the cross-\ufb01rm variation is now purged. Figure 3 documented signi\ufb01cant di\ufb00erences in AI exposure across occupations. This raises the concern that our results may be confounded by secular trends across broad\n\n[...]\n\n in sales and administration, two of the broad occupational categories that have been in decline for other reasons (e.g., Autor and Dorn, 2013). These controls do not substantially change the estimate in either column, which remain, respectively, at 9.75 (se = 1.20) and 16.87 (se = 1.86).18 The \ufb01rst panel of Figure 5 shows the speci\ufb01cation from column 3 in the form of a bin scatterplot, where each bin represents about 50,000 establishments. The relationship be- tween AI exposure and AI vacancy postings is fairly close to linear acr\n\n[...]\n\ncomple- mentary visualization, depicting the evolution of AI vacancies for the four quartiles of the establishment AI exposure measure. It shows that the top two quartiles post signi\ufb01cantly more AI vacancies and drive the surge in AI vacancies after 2015.19 18Table A1 in the Appendix shows that the results in Panel A are also robust if we include the baseline shares of ten broad occupational categories. For example, the coe\ufb03cients in the speci\ufb01cations that parallel columns 5 and 6 are, respectively, 7.24 (se = 1.44) and 13.70 (se = 2.12). However, some of the results for the Webb and SML AI exposure measures are sensitive to these controls. 19Our exposure measure is a \u201cshift-share\u201d instrument, and the heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors are not the most conservative ones beca\n\n[...]\n\nitial occupation composition by estimating a version of our main regression equation (6), with the share of establishment vacancies in each detailed occupation in 2010-2012 as regressors. The adjusted R2 of the Felten et al measure of AI exposure is 0.0256. The adjusted R2 when initial occupation shares are used as regressors is 0.10.20 Hence, our AI exposure measure accounts for more than one-quarter of the AI adoption associated with baseline occupation structures. Results with the SML index in Panel C are broadly similar, but signi\ufb01cantly weaker. There is a positive association between the SML-based measure of AI exposure and AI vacanc\n\n[...]\n\ntal technologies. The inclusion of the software exposure measure has little impact on the coe\ufb03cients of interest, particularly in the case of the Felten et al. index. The software exposure measure itself does not have a consistent association with AI vacancy growth: it is positive and statistically signi\ufb01cant in some speci\ufb01cations, small and insigni\ufb01cant in others, and negative and signi\ufb01cant in yet o",

Saito\u00a7 October 6, 2022 Abstract This paper studies the relationship between industrial robots and employment in Japan based on a unique dataset \n\n[...]\n\nnstructing a Bartik-style industry-year-level robot price index to estimate the impact of robot price on robot adoption and employment. In our empirical analysis, we show that a one percent decrease in the price of robots increased robot adoption by 1.54 percent. Perhaps more surprisingly, we also \ufb01nd that a one percent decrease in the robot price increased employment by 0.44 percent, so a large availability of robots actually raised employment, suggesting that the scale e\ufb00ect induced by robot adoption was substantial and dominated the substitution e\ufb00ect. As we found a large and signi\ufb01cant elasticity of industrial real o\n\n[...]\n\necond we regressed the changes in labor market outcomes on the number of robots using the instrumental variable of the shift-share measure of robot prices across CZs. Our results indicate that one robot unit per 1,000 workers increases employment by 2.2 percent, corroborating our industry-level \ufb01nding that a decline in robot prices increases employment. This contrasts with the \ufb01nding by AR, whose corresponding estimate was -1.6 percent. We also \ufb01nd that the across- industry-within-region spillovers are limited, as the positive employment e\ufb00ect was predominantly found in the manufacturing sector but not in the non-manufacturing sector or for those who were not employed and \n\n[...]\n\n the change in labor demand is characterized by two e\ufb00ects, (i) a shift in the task allocation from labor to robots and (ii) a cost savings due to EAR. Finally, we use the change in the robot price as a proxy for EAR to derive a regression equation. 2.1 Setup We consider an economy with T periods indexed by t = 0, 1, . . .,T and I industries indexed by i = 1, . . ., I. Production factors are labor and robots, where robots are de\ufb01ned as the combination of robots used in each application, as describe\n\n[...]\n\nat even under this assumption, the price of robots remains relevant for factor demand. Speci\ufb01cally, as we see in the following sections, labor demand is a\ufb00ected by both the task-based force (automation) and the standard force (factor price changes). 2.2 Solution Solving this model involves the characterization of the following producer task allocation problem. Let us focus on industry i in year t. Since tasks are ordered according to robots\u2019 comparative advantage, the optimal task allocation can be\n\n[...]\n\n task by robots or workers. tests\fixtures\drb72\evidence_pool.json:267:    "direct_quote": "Elevator pitch Artificial intelligence (AI) has streamlined processes, improved workforce allocation, and created new jobs to meet the needs of digitalization and automation. Individuals with AI capital experience greater employment opportunities and higher wages, particularly in high-skilled roles and large firms. Training in AI helps reduce gender-based digital disparities, empowers individuals, and enhances their employability. Policymakers should promote inclusive AI development policies to prevent widening AI-related divides and unemployment, and to ensure equitable opportunities. Key findings Pros Investments in education and skills related to AI have boosted employment, particularly in high-skilled occupations. AI adoption has led to higher demand for AI-related skills and wage premiums, especially in high-skilled occupations. AI has increased employee productivity for less-experienced and lower-skilled employees. AI helps industries weather economic downturns by stabilizing employment levels. AI enhances job quality for historically disadvantaged groups by reducing reliance on physical strength and promoting cognitive skills. Cons AI increases job displacement risks for low-skilled, routine employees, widening the gap between high- and low-skilled employees. AI-investing firms favor highly educated employees, reducing non-degree roles and some non-technical jobs. AI might worsen income inequality, especially impacting low-skilled employees. Employees\u2019 distrust of work\n\n[...]\n\nnomies are likely to experience more significant effects from AI automation than emerging markets, due to differences in labor market composition and industrial structures [[9]](izawol.514.ref9), [[10]](izawol.514.ref10). In the UK in 2020, at least 42.5% of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) utilized AI in their operations [[2]](izawol.514.ref2). As observed in [Figure 1](#IZAWOL.514.chart1), UK SMEs used AI for various operations, such as collecting information related to customers' online \n\n[...]\n\now AI is associated with labor market outcomes, with the magnitude of AI\u2019s impact varying across studies. For instance, in the US, between 2010 and 2018, a one-standard-deviation increase in investments in education and skills related to AI led to a 19.5% increase in sales, an 18.1% increase in employment, and a 22.3% increase in market valuation [[3]](izawol.514.ref3). Additionally, between 2010 and 2019, there was a notable rise in the demand for AI skills across US industries, accompanied by significant wage premiums, particularly in high-skilled occupations \n\n[...]\n\nterns highlight AI's potential to drive employment and growth by enabling more efficient production and fostering new industries [[9]](izawol.514.ref9). Indeed, predictions suggest that AI has the potential to increase global GDP growth by 7% and by 1.5% in the US, demonstrating substantial macroeconomic potential if AI adoption becomes widespread [[9]](izawol.514.ref9). More modest predictions in the US indicate that AI is projected to increase total factor productivity by no more than 0.66% [[11]](izawol.514.ref11). At a micro level, in the UK in 2022, job applicants with AI capital experienced approximately 14.4% higher wage prospects compared to those without AI capital [[1]](izawol.514.ref1). The [illustration](#IZAWOL.514.ga) shows that in business administration occupations, the wage premium was the highest in the sample, followed by accounting occupations and economics occupations. Similarly, as observed in [Figure 2](#IZAWOL.514.chart2), job applicants with [AI capital](#izawol.694-div-371874) had, on average, a 22.3 percentage point higher chance of receiving interview invitations than those without AI capital [[1]](izawol.514.ref1). tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:5:Weight reduction is the most consistently observed outcome across IF trials, and it is mechanistically central to the improvement of insulin resistance in T2DM. In adults with overweight or obesity, IF reduced fat mass by a mean difference of −0.72 kg compared with a non-intervention diet [CITE:ev_243de27dc515c169]. When IF is combined with structured physical exercise, the weight reduction relative to a control diet plus exercise reaches a weighted mean difference of −1.83 kg (P = 0.001) [CITE:ev_749f00cb5c5eea28], suggesting an additive effect when caloric timing is paired with energy expenditure. The largest effect size is observed with modified alternate-day fasting, which produced a weight reduction of −5.18 kg (95% CI: −7.04 to −3.32) compared with a usual diet, supported by high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_e7bdb4797d3f6f0f]. The differential between these protocols is clinically meaningful: a five-kilogram loss falls within the range associated with measurable improvements in HbA1c and insulin sensitivity, whereas sub-kilogram fat-mass reductions, although statistically significant, may yield more modest glycemic benefits.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:11:The lipid response to IF reinforces its cardiometabolic relevance for the T2DM population, where dyslipidemia is a frequent comorbidity. Across umbrella-review syntheses, IF reduced low-density lipoprotein cholesterol by a standardized mean difference of −0.20 (95% CI: −0.38 to −0.02; p = 0.027) compared with a non-intervention diet [CITE:ev_410655e66705beb6]. When IF is combined with exercise, the reduction is larger and reported in absolute units: a weighted mean difference of −5.35 mg/dL (P = 0.03) for LDL cholesterol [CITE:ev_39b4891376876856]. Triacylglycerols also fall under IF protocols, with a standardized mean difference of −0.23 (95% CI: −0.39 to −0.06; p = 0.007) [CITE:ev_e273c28dab55ecd8]. The convergence of LDL and triglyceride reductions is consistent with the broader observation that IF exerts beneficial effects on the lipid profile [CITE:ev_dde1177bb61ef9e9], a finding that strengthens its candidacy as an adjunctive intervention for individuals with T2DM whose treatment goals extend beyond glycemic control to encompass cardiovascular risk attenuation.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:32:Blood pressure reduction is a parallel mechanism through which IF reduces overall cardiometabolic burden in T2DM. Systolic blood pressure fell by a weighted mean difference of −2.99 mm Hg (P = 0.003) when IF was combined with exercise versus control diet plus exercise [CITE:ev_1ece74de7d460b0e]. The effect is substantially larger under modified alternate-day fasting, where systolic blood pressure decreased by −7.24 mmHg (95% CI: −11.90 to −2.58) compared with usual diet, again with high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_47ea3711836ebb90]. 1:"""
2:Multi-section generator — HONEST-REBUILD Gap-4.
3:
4:Three-stage architecture that produces 1500-3000-word reports while
5:keeping per-section provenance tightness:
6:
7:  1. OUTLINE stage  (1 LLM call, ~500 tokens)
8:     DeepSeek reads all evidence and emits a JSON section plan:
9:       [{"title": "Efficacy", "focus": "...", "ev_ids": ["ev_001", ...]},
10:        {"title": "Safety", "focus": "...", "ev_ids": [...]},
11:        {"title": "Comparative", ...}]
12:     Sections constrained to a fixed allowed set so the model can't
13:     invent topics unsupported by evidence.
14:
15:  2. PER-SECTION GENERATION  (N parallel LLM calls, ~800 tokens each)
16:     Each section gets its own prompt with ONLY its evidence subset +
17:     focus statement. Generates 8-15 sentences with [ev_XXX] markers.
18:
19:  3. VERIFY + OPTIONAL REGEN  (deterministic + 0-N retry calls)
20:     Each section is strict_verified. If <50% sentences kept, the
21:     section is regenerated ONCE with a "tighter citations required"
22:     reminder. If regen still fails, the section is dropped (with a
23:     note in the report).
24:
25:  4. ASSEMBLY
26:     verified_sections + shared Methods + contradictions + Limitations
27:     + bibliography, concatenated.
28:
29:Cost estimate: ~$0.01-$0.02 per report (vs $0.0022 for single-call).
30:"""

codex
I’m going to make the minimal contract change now: reducer output will be normalized back to legacy `[ev_XXX]` citations before `_rewrite_draft_with_spans`, so final spans are computed from the final sentence the same way legacy does. This keeps strict_verify unchanged and removes the reducer’s dependency on pre-bound narrow MAP spans.
The divergent contract is now clear enough to patch: distill bypasses the legacy sentence-aware span binder by carrying reducer-supplied full tokens into strict_verify.
  ✓ Read distiller, generator, and strict verifier paths
  ✓ Trace citation/span transformations in legacy vs distill
  → Confirm/refute suspects with line evidence
  • Identify one root cause and fix

5:---------------
6:The legacy per-section generation path hands V4 Pro the raw ``<<<evidence:...>>>``
7:quote blocks plus an atom catalog and asks it to write cited prose in ONE pass.
8:The reasoning-first writer then has to (a) read every quote, (b) decide what is
9:section-relevant, (c) keep numbers exact, and (d) emit ``[#ev:...]`` provenance
10:tokens â€” all at once. The drb_72 / drb_76 forensic sweeps showed this conflated
11:job leaks: the writer over-claims, mis-binds numbers across sources, and
12:``strict_verify`` then DROPS the over-claimed sentences (drb_76 baseline: 40
13:verified / 41 dropped, 29 of the drops being entailment failures). The faithful
14:content is there in the corpus; the single-pass writer just cannot hold it.
15:
16:THE FIX (map-reduce)
17:--------------------
18:1. MAP â€” one LLM call PER SOURCE distills the source into atomic, span-grounded
19:   findings. Each finding is LOCALLY VALIDATED fail-closed against the SAME
20:   production verifier the section gate uses (``verify_sentence_provenance`` with
21:   ``require_number_match=True``, which enforces numbers-in-span AND entailment).
22:   A finding that cannot be validated is rejected here, before it can pollute the
23:   ledger. Every input source yields >=1 accepted finding OR a ``CoverageRow`` so
24:   no source ever silently disappears (LAW II fail-loud).
25:2. REDUCE â€” the section writer composes prose over the VALIDATED findings ledger
26:   only (reference-first). It never sees raw quotes; it can only cite findings
27:   that already passed the gate. The output still carries the original
28:   ``[#ev:evidence_id:start-end]`` token for every factual sentence, so the
29:   downstream ``_rewrite_draft_with_spans`` (unchanged) and ``strict_verify``
30:   (unchanged) re-check every sentence exactly as today â€” the distiller TIGHTENS,
31:   it never relaxes, the faithfulness gates.
32:
33:FAITHFULNESS INVARIANT
34:----------------------
35:This module NEVER weakens a gate. A distilled finding is only ever a CANDIDATE
36:that has ALREADY passed the production verifier on its own; the final section
37:prose is re-verified by the unchanged ``strict_verify`` exactly as the legacy
38:path is. The distiller can only REDUCE what the writer is allowed to assert.
39:
40:Flag-gated entirely by ``PG_SECTION_DISTILL`` (read in the multi_section seam).
41:This module is only imported when that flag is ON; when OFF the legacy path is
42:byte-identical (no import, no call, no prompt change).
43:"""

61:    verify_sentence_provenance,
62:)

65:

67:# entries from an older distiller can never be replayed against a new contract.
68:DISTILLER_VERSION = "section_distiller_v1"
69:

codex
The prompt/cache contract is now switched to legacy citation markers and the distiller cache version is bumped so older full-token reducer outputs are not replayed. Next I’m changing the marker filter to accept `[ev_XXX]` and defensively downgrade any old full tokens back to `[ev_XXX]` before the span binder sees them.

+_BARE_EV_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"\[(?P<ev_id>[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)\]")
+_DUP_BARE_EV_MARKER_RE = re.compile(
+    r"(\[(?P<ev_id>[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)\])(?:\s+\[(?P=ev_id)\])+"
+)
 
 
 def filter_and_strip_reduce_markers(
     raw: str,
     distillate: SectionDistillate,
 ) -> str:
-    """Post-process the REDUCE output before the UNCHANGED _rewrite_draft_with_spans
-    and strict_verify run.

-        them; the [#ev:...] token is what strict_verify re-checks).
